policy_statement: By 2022, study the role of Muni fare programs on equity, climate, and mobility goals and adopt recommendations (TLU.1-7).
mechanism_description: The city will study Muni fare programs and adopt recommendations intended to improve equity and mobility outcomes and support lower transportation emissions through increased public transit use.
primary_causal_pathway: Fare/pricing policy → Mode shift (car/VMT → transit) → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The city adjusts transit fares and fare programs (e.g., discounts, fare capping, free/low-cost passes, integrated fares) to alter the generalized cost of using public transit.  
(2) Process: Lower relative cost and improved affordability increase transit ridership and reduce private vehicle use and vehicle-miles traveled (VMT); this reduces gasoline/diesel combustion (and may also reduce congestion-related inefficiency).  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in transportation-sector GHG emissions (and co-benefits such as improved air quality); magnitude depends on mode-shift share and transit system capacity/energy mix.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions caused by fare changes that shift trips from private vehicles to public transit.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Pricing/affordability interventions → increased transit usage → reduced private car travel → reduced fuel combustion → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although fare programs often target equity/access, the principal climate mechanism referenced and generally expected is emissions mitigation through modal shift; it is not primarily about preparing for climate hazards (adaptation), reducing resource use for the same service without necessarily changing modes (resource efficiency), or ecosystem processes (nature-based).  
3) Edge cases applied: If fare reductions increase total travel demand without displacing car trips, mitigation benefits could be smaller; if additional transit service is added with high-emitting energy sources, benefits depend on net per-passenger emissions. These do not change the dominant intended mechanism across the class.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any “transit fare policy optimization → modal shift → emissions reduction” mechanism inherently targets emissions reductions via travel behavior change, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., pricing instrument type, equity-focused design, integration with service improvements) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/induced demand or limited mode-shift (e.g., shifts from walking/biking rather than cars) can weaken mitigation impact; net emissions also depend on transit vehicle energy mix and capacity constraints. These are implementation contingencies rather than a different primary mechanism.
key_indicators: “By 2022, study the role of Muni fare programs”; “adopt recommendations”; “equity, climate, and mobility goals”
co_benefits: Equity/affordability for low-income riders; improved mobility/access; potential air-quality improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a future pricing-mechanism (fare restructuring) action, but the verbatim text specifies studying and adopting recommendations (planning/evaluation) rather than implementing a concrete fare change.
